#

## Overview

It is important for Slurm to know that the Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs)
that it receives are coming from trusted sources. There are a few different
authentication mechanisms available within Slurm to verify the legitimacy and
integrity of the requests.

If you plan to change the authentication type, you will need to drain the
cluster of running jobs and stop all Slurm daemons before making the change.
Otherwise, requests can be made with the new type while the running daemons
still operate with the old type that was present when they started.

## MUNGE

MUNGE can be used to create and validate credentials. It allows Slurm to
authenticate the UID and GID of a request from another host that has matching
users and groups. MUNGE libraries must exist when building Slurm in order for
it to be able to use munge for authentication. All hosts in the cluster must
have a shared cryptographic key.

#### Setup

1. MUNGE requires that there be a shared key on the machines running
   slurmctld, slurmdbd and the nodes. You can create a key file by entering your
   own text or by generating random data:

   ```
   dd if=/dev/random of=/etc/munge/munge.key bs=1024 count=1
   ```
2. This key should be owned by the "munge" user and should not be readable
   or writable by other users:

   ```
   chown munge:munge /etc/munge/munge.key
   chmod 400 /etc/munge/munge.key
   ```
3. Distribute the key file to the machines on the cluster. It needs to be
   on the machines running slurmctld, slurmdbd, slurmd and any submit hosts
   you have configured. Use the distribution method of your choice.
4. The "munge" service should be running on any machines that need to use it
   for authentication. It should be enabled and started on all the machines you
   distributed a key to:

   ```
   systemctl enable munge
   systemctl start munge
   ```
5. Changing the authentication mechanism requires a restart of Slurm daemons.
   The daemons need to be stopped before updating the slurm.conf so that client
   commands do not use a mechanism other than what the running daemons are
   expecting.
6. Update your slurm.conf and slurmdbd.conf to use MUNGE authentication.
   * slurm.conf:

     ```
     AuthType = auth/munge
     CredType = cred/munge
     ```
   * slurmdbd.conf:

     ```
     AuthType = auth/munge
     ```
7. Start the Slurm daemons back up with the appropriate method for your
   cluster.

## Slurm

Beginning with version 23.11, Slurm has its own plugin that can create and
validate credentials. It validates that the requests come from legitimate UIDs
and GIDs on other hosts with matching users and groups. All hosts in the
cluster must have a shared cryptographic key.

#### Single Key Setup

1. For the authentication to happen correctly you must have a shared key on
   the machine running slurmctld, slurmdbd and the nodes. You can create a key
   file by entering your own text or by generating random data:

   ```
   dd if=/dev/random of=/etc/slurm/slurm.key bs=1024 count=1
   ```

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

### Step 3: Scan the QR code or enter the secret key.[¶](#step-3-scan-the-qr-code-or-enter-the-secret-key "Permalink to this headline")

In your chosen authenticator app, you will want to create a new entry for your CSD3 SSH Login TOTP. In this walkthrough, we use Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator as examples.

  inherent trust of that user and it then uses `auth/munge` or
  `auth/slurm` for everything after that.
* [JWKS](#external_auth)

  This is like an authentication proxy, as another system is used to create the
  tokens, but it skips having the authentication system in front of Slurm by using
  signed public keys. This tends to be the preferred solution for sites using
  cloud authentication systems, such as:

  + [Keycloak](https://www.keycloak.org/) - Using keycloak is an
    option which doesn't require a cloud auth solution.

## Setup for Standalone Use

2. Add the same JWT key to both the controller and slurmdbd (if used). For the
   controller only, it is recommended to put the JWT key in the StateSaveLocation.
   For example, using /var/spool/slurm/statesave/:

   ```
   dd if=/dev/random of=/var/spool/slurm/statesave/jwt_hs256.key bs=32 count=1
   chown slurm:slurm /var/spool/slurm/statesave/jwt_hs256.key
   chmod 0600 /var/spool/slurm/statesave/jwt_hs256.key
   chown slurm:slurm /var/spool/slurm/statesave
   chmod 0755 /var/spool/slurm/statesave
   ```

   The key does not have to be in the StateSaveLocation, but that is a convenient
   location if you have multiple controllers since it is shared between them.
   The key should not be placed in a directory where non-admin users might be
   able to access it.
   The key file should be owned by **SlurmUser** or **root**, with
   recommended permissions of 0400. The file must not be accessible by 'other'.
3. In both slurm.conf and slurmdbd.conf, add JWT as an alternative
   authentication type:

   ```
   AuthAltTypes=auth/jwt
   AuthAltParameters=jwt_key=/var/spool/slurm/statesave/jwt_hs256.key
   ```
4. Restart slurmctld
5. Create tokens for users as desired:

   ```
   scontrol token username=$USER
   ```

   An optional **lifespan=$LIFESPAN** option can be used to change the token
   lifespan from the default 1800 seconds. The root account, or **SlurmUser**
   account can be used to generate tokens for any user. Alternatively, a user
   may use the command to generate tokens for themselves by simply calling

   ```
   scontrol token
   ```

   Note that administrators can prevent users from generating tokens by setting
   the following parameter in slurm.conf:

   ```
   AuthAltParameters=disable_token_creation
   ```

   This functionality is provided to allow sites to control when and how users are
   provided tokens along with controlling the token lifespans.
6. Export the **SLURM\_JWT** environment variable before calling any Slurm
   command.

user_input:
Hello,
I hope this email finds you well!
I am working on CSD3 for some cpu jobs, particularly nodes cclake and Icelake. I submitted some jobs last week and I found the queueing time very long. I am wondering if you can give advice on this regarding if there is anything I did wrong causing the long wait. Some general info:
- Name: Hongyu Zhou
- Account/PI: N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
- Working directory: /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report
- Task:
- Data: I work with bibliographic data on hundreds of millions of paper including their title, abstract, and citation relationships
- Task:
- I am trying to calculate the cooccurence frequency of concepts in papers (two concepts used in one paper), for which the concepts are extracted already in our current data (one paper associated with a list of paper).
- I am building these cooccurrence networks for using papers since 1990 to current and group them to a interval of 3 years
- I generate networks for every three years.
- Problem:
- The queueing time is quite long.
- Slurm Job_id=10784619 Name=build_cooccur_net Began, Queued time 17:19:49
- I tried to minimise the potential RAM usage by using smaller intervals, using smaller samples and use sql-like ducked to calculate the concurrences. But in the future, there will definitely be larger jobs I need to run considering the size of my data in nature, especially if we were use Node2Vec to embed the networks we obtained.
- Job submission script:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J build_cooccur_net
#SBATCH -A N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=6
#SBATCH --mem=30G
#SBATCH --time=4:00:00
#SBATCH --output=1_build_net_duck_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=1_build_net_duck_%j.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=hz488@cam.ac.uk
set -euo pipefail
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job started"
module purge
module load python/3.8
source "$HOME/hzenv/bin/activate"
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
export OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1
TEMP_DIR="${SLURM_TMPDIR:-./temp_edges}"
mkdir -p "$TEMP_DIR"
python /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report/scripts/1_build_net_save_duckdb.py \
--input-dir /home/hz488/project/dimensions/publications/ \
--output-dir /home/hz488/project/data/ai_ranking_report/data/1_build_net \
--batch-size 200000 \
--batches-per-file 50 \
--sample-frac 1
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job finished”
Could you kindly advise me on this?
Best,
Hongyu

Category: Scheduling / Queue Wait Times / Service Levels
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on non-paying SL3 CPU saw long waits. Correct explanation: paid workloads have higher priority; queue times fluctuate; cancelling/resubmitting loses accrued priority.

ACTION
1) Reassure no job/account fault detected.
2) Explain SL3 prioritisation and variable wait times.
3) Advise against cancel/resubmit behavior.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Hongyu,

From the information provided, there is no obvious sign of a problem with your account or submission. Your jobs are on the non-paying SL3 service level, and paid activity is prioritised ahead of it.

Queue time can vary significantly. Please avoid cancelling/resubmitting, as that can lose accrued priority.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Avoid promising specific start-time estimates on shared queues.

The user is experiencing long queue times for their jobs on the CSD3 cluster, specifically on the icelake and cclake nodes [1]. 4. Advise the user to check their job's dependencies, constraints, and QOS settings, as these may be contributing to the long queue times [3][6].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)

- Have you tried submitting your job to a different partition or node type?
- Are there any specific error messages or warnings in your job's output or error files?

## Submitting long jobs[¶](#submitting-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Use of QOSL is tied to the -long partitions, therefore once given access it is necessary only to specify this partition - e.g.

# Job Reason Codes

These reason codes can be used to identify why a pending job has not yet been
started by the scheduler. There may be multiple reasons why a job cannot start,
in which case only the reason that was encountered by the attempted scheduling
method will be displayed. Refer to the [Scheduling Configuration Guide](sched_config.html) for more details.

* **AssocGrp\*** — The job's association has reached an aggregate
  [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **AssocMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a maximum
  [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for the
  requested association.
* **BeginTime** — The job's earliest [start time](sbatch.html#OPT_begin) has not yet been reached.
* **Dependency** — This job has a
  [dependency](sbatch.html#OPT_dependency) on another job that has not
  been satisfied.
* **Max\*PerAccount** — A portion of the job request exceeds the
  per-Account [limit](resource_limits.html) on the job's
  [QOS](qos.html).
* **Priority** — One of more higher
  [priority](priority_multifactor.html) jobs exist for the partition
  associated with the job or for the [advanced
  reservation](reservations.html).
* **QOSGrp\*** — The job's [QOS](qos.html) has reached an
  aggregate [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **QOSMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a
  maximum [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for
  the requested [QOS](qos.html).
* **Resources** — The resources requested by the job are not
  available (e.g., already used by other jobs).

```

These are comments to bash, but are interpreted by SLURM as requests to use 1 node, with 76 tasks in total (which because each Ice Lake node has 76 cores, completely utilizes one node), for 2 hours of wall time (i.e. actual time as measured by a clock on the wall, rather than CPU time). Finally, the following command submits the job to the queueing system:

```
sbatch slurm_submit
```

Please note that each Ice Lake node has 76 physical CPU cores, and one should normally be careful not to start more working processes or threads per node than there are CPUs per node (note that hyperthreading is disabled, so there is no distinction between “CPU core” and “CPU”).

#!/bin/bash
#!
#! Example SLURM job script for Peta4-Skylake (Skylake CPUs, OPA)
#! Last updated: Mon 13 Nov 12:25:17 GMT 2017
#!
#!#############################################################
#!#### Modify the options in this section as appropriate ######
#!#############################################################
#! sbatch directives begin here ###############################
#! Name of the job:
#SBATCH -J runDown100
#! Which project should be charged:
#SBATCH -A DIRAC-DP213-CPU
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=32
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total? (<= nodes*32)
#! The skylake/skylake-himem nodes have 32 CPUs (cores) each.
#SBATCH --ntasks=1024
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=23:50:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
#SBATCH --mail-type=END
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
#SBATCH --no-requeue
#! For 6GB per CPU, set "-p skylake"; for 12GB per CPU, set "-p skylake-himem": 
#SBATCH -p skylake
#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)
#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by core number*walltime.
#! The --ntasks value refers to the number of tasks to be launched by SLURM only. This
#! usually equates to the number of MPI tasks launched. Reduce this from nodes*32 if
#! demanded by memory requirements, or if OMP_NUM_THREADS>1.
#! Each task is allocated 1 core by default, and each core is allocated 5980MB (skylake)
#! and 12030MB (skylake-himem). If this is insufficient, also specify
#! --cpus-per-task and/or --mem (the latter specifies MB per node).
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e  's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! ############################################################
#! Modify the settings below to specify the application's environment, location 
#! and launch method:
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh                # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge
module load rhel7/default-peta4
module unload intel/compilers/2017.4 intel/mkl/2017.4 intel/impi/2017.4/intel intel/libs/idb/2017.4 intel/libs/tbb/2017.4 intel/libs/ipp/2017.4 intel/libs/daal/2017.4 intel/bundles/complib/2017.4 
module load gcc/9 openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4 
module list
#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed:
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/rds/user/dc-mort1/rds-dirac-dp213-EMGQCSMFaDU/scalingTests/GVB_C700_l100n1024_SLEGAC/bar_gizmo/libgrackle/grackle-3.1.1/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/home/dc-mort1/software/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
#! Full path to application executable: 
application="gizmo-tmox/GIZMO"
#application="/home/dc-onor1/test/ng4b/Gadget4"
#! Run options for the application:
options="gizmo.param"
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR"  # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 32:
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
#! The following variables define a sensible pinning strategy for Intel MPI tasks -
#! this should be suitable for both pure MPI and hybrid MPI/OpenMP jobs:
export I_MPI_PIN_DOMAIN=omp:compact # Domains are $OMP_NUM_THREADS cores in size
export I_MPI_PIN_ORDER=scatter # Adjacent domains have minimal sharing of caches/sockets
#! Notes:
#! 1. These variables influence Intel MPI only.
#! 2. Domains are non-overlapping sets of cores which map 1-1 to MPI tasks.
#! 3. I_MPI_PIN_PROCESSOR_LIST is ignored if I_MPI_PIN_DOMAIN is set.
#! 4. If MPI tasks perform better when sharing caches/sockets, try I_MPI_PIN_ORDER=compact.
#! Uncomment one choice for CMD below (add mpirun/mpiexec options if necessary):
#! Choose this for a MPI code (possibly using OpenMP) using Intel MPI.
#CMD="mpirun -ppn $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np $application $options"
#! Choose this for a pure shared-memory OpenMP parallel program on a single node:
#! (OMP_NUM_THREADS threads will be created):
#CMD="$application $options"
#! Choose this for a MPI code (possibly using OpenMP) using OpenMPI:
CMD="mpirun -npernode $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np $application $options"
###############################################################
1. 1. 1. You should not have to change anything below this line ####
###############################################################
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID\n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
if [ "$SLURM_JOB_NODELIST" ]; then
#! Create a machine file:
export NODEFILE=`generate_pbs_nodefile`
cat $NODEFILE | uniq > machine.file.$JOBID
echo -e "\nNodes allocated:\n================"
echo `cat machine.file.$JOBID | sed -e 's/\..*$//g'`
fi
echo -e "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
echo -e "\nExecuting command:\n==================\n$CMD\n"
eval $CMD

/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3

Copying the appropriate example file and then modifying the top section (where indicated) will create a script suitable for submission to the batch queueing system via the command sbatch.

There are also examples of submission scripts for specific applications in this documentation - see for example [CASTEP](/hpc/software-packages/castep.html) or [LAMMPS](/hpc/software-packages/lammps.html).

Peta4 and Wilkes3 operate the SLURM batch queueing system for managing resources. Some useful commands:

```
squeue      -> show global cluster information
sinfo       -> show global cluster information
sview       -> show global cluster information
scontrol show job nnnn -> examine the job with jobid nnnn
scontrol show node nodename -> examine the node with name nodename
sbatch      -> submits an executable script to the queueing system
sintr       -> submits an interactive job to the queueing system
srun        -> run a command either as a new job or within an existing job
scancel     -> delete a job
mybalance   -> show current balance of core hour credits
```

Once your application is compiled, e.g. to a binary called *prog*, it can be submitted to the queueing system as follows (we assume it is destined for Ice Lake).

Firstly, copy the template SLURM submission script:

```
cp /usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-icelake slurm_submit
```

user_input:
Dear HPC team,
Good afternoon, I am contacting you since I am trying to run Freesurfer 8.0.0 on HPC but I am getting the following error:
FREESURFER: Undefined variable.
This makes me wonder if for some reason the path to freesurfer cannot be found. Since this is a new module can you please advise on the path where the application is so that I can use in my scripts? Has this been tested and was running as intended?
Needless to say I have loaded the module.
Best wishes,
Marialena
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Module Integration / Environment Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes (module/setup correction)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User loaded `freesurfer/8.0.0` and received `FREESURFER: Undefined variable`. From the original query, the most likely issue is incomplete environment setup after loading the module rather than the software being absent. The next step is to reproduce the module load, check the exact command or script the user is running, and then handle any further setup requirements such as the FreeSurfer setup script or licence configuration if they arise.

ACTION
1) Reproduce the issue and validate module behaviour.
2) Ask the user to show the exact commands or job script they are using.
3) Instruct the standard setup sequence:
   - `module load freesurfer/8.0.0`
   - `source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh`
4) If a subsequent error indicates a missing licence file, instruct the user to obtain a valid licence and set `FS_LICENSE=/path/to/license.txt`.
5) Confirm successful execution after the environment is set correctly.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Marialena,

Please first try the standard setup sequence:

module load freesurfer/8.0.0
source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh

Then retry the command that is failing.

If you then receive a separate licence-file error, please send that exact message and we can advise on setting `FS_LICENSE` correctly.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not request users place the licence file in protected system directories.
- Do not assume the initial undefined-variable error proves the software is missing or broken.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with running Freesurfer 8.0.0 on HPC, receiving an error message indicating an undefined variable [3]. This suggests that the path to Freesurfer may not be correctly set or the module may not be properly loaded [3][7]. The user has loaded the module, but the issue persists [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, please try the following steps:
1. Run `module av freesurfer` to check the availability of the Freesurfer module [3].
2. Load the Freesurfer module using `module load freesurfer/8.0.0` [3].
3. Verify that the Freesurfer executable is in the system's PATH environment variable [7].
4. If the issue persists, try running `freesurfer --help` to check if the executable is working correctly [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide the output of `module list` to confirm which modules are currently loaded [7]?
- Have you tried running Freesurfer on a different node or with a different version of the module [3]?

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- It is essential to use the correct version of the module and ensure that it is properly loaded to avoid conflicts or errors [3][7].
- The user should be aware of the module's documentation and usage guidelines to ensure correct usage [7].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the issue with running Freesurfer 8.0.0 on HPC, please try the steps outlined above. If you have any further questions or concerns, please do not hesitate to contact us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

Set your environment:

```
$ sudo apt update
$ sudo apt install tcl
$ sudo apt install environment-modules
```

Confirm that the local copy of `tclsh` is new enough (see the beginning of this page for a list of supported versions):

```
$ echo 'puts [info patchlevel] ; exit 0' | tclsh
8.6.8
```

Test the `module` installation by initializing the `module` alias.

```
$ source /usr/share/modules/init/sh
$ module
```

NOTE:

Initialization of the Modulefiles environment in POSIX-compatible shells should work with the source command shown above. However, the precise location and name of the modulefiles init folder does vary with the Linux distribution. Most installations of Environment Modules will automatically source the modulefiles initialization script: `/etc/profile.d/modules.sh`. You can test for proper initialization of the module command by typing the `module --version` command which should result in a response similar to: `Modules Release 4.4.1 (2020-01-03)`. At this point, the system should be ready to use the module command as shown in the following section.

### Getting Started with the `modulefiles-setup.sh` Script

The following example assumes you have:

* installed `tclsh` on to the Linux development system
* installed the Environment Modules utility (i.e., `module`) onto the system
* sourced the `.../modules/init/sh` (or equivalent) module init command
* installed the oneAPI toolkits required for your oneAPI development

Navigate to the installation directory, and load `tbb`:

```
$ cd <oneapi-root-folder>        # cd to the oneapi_root installation directory
$ ./modulefiles-setup.sh         # run the modulefiles setup script
$ module use modulefiles         # use the modulefiles folder created above
$ module avail                   # will show tbb/X.Y, etc.
$ module load tbb                # loads tbb/X.Y module
$ module list                    # should list the tbb/X.Y module you just loaded
```

* Use the `env` command to inspect the environment and look for the changes that were made by the modulefile you loaded.

```
$ env | grep -i "intel"
```

For example, if you loaded the `tbb modulefile`, the command will show you some of the env changes made by that `modulefile`.

* Unload `tbb`:

```
$ module unload tbb              # removes tbb/X.Y changes from the environment
$ module list                    # should no longer list the tbb/X.Y env var module
```

NOTE:

A `modulefile` is a script, but it does not need to have the ‘x’ (executable) permission set, because it is loaded and interpreted by the “module” interpreter that is installed and maintained by the end-user. Installation of the oneAPI toolkits do not include the `modulefile` interpreter. It must be installed separately. Likewise, `modulefiles` do not require that the ‘w’ permission be set, but they must be readable (ideally, the ‘r’ permission is set for all users).

### Versioning

`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4

icc: warning #10352: The directory '/tmp' is full.  Please check disk space.
I didn't have any issues trying to compile the code a few days ago. According to "quota":

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login Node Health / Temporary Filesystem
Technical Action Required: Yes (diagnose node-specific issue)
Escalation Required: Optional (if persistent)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports compiler warning that `/tmp` is full and asks whether this relates to their own quota. `/tmp` is node-local temporary storage and can fill independently of the user’s `/home` or project quota.

ACTION
1) Explain what `/tmp` is:
   - temporary local filesystem on the node,
   - not the same as user/project quota shown by `quota`.
2) Ask user to report the node where error occurs (`hostname`).
3) Suggest immediate workaround: try compiling on another login node.
4) Escalate if issue persists across multiple nodes or for multiple users.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Loren,

The warning about `/tmp` means temporary space on the current node is full. This is separate from your `/home`/project quota, so your `quota` output can still look normal.

Please share the output of:
`hostname`

As a workaround, please try compiling on a different login node. If the issue appears on multiple nodes, let us know and we will escalate.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume user quota issue when warning explicitly references `/tmp`.

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The error message "The directory '/tmp' is full.  Please check disk space." indicates that the temporary directory is full, which is preventing the compilation of the code [3][5]. This issue has been encountered before, and the solution was to clear space in the /tmp directory [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate which node the user is working on to identify the specific /tmp directory that is full.
2. Clear space in the /tmp directory to resolve the issue [3][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm which node you are seeing /tmp full?
- Are you using any specific scripts or commands that may be contributing to the /tmp directory being full?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
The issue you are experiencing is due to the /tmp directory being full. We will investigate and clear space in the /tmp directory to resolve the issue. Please confirm which node you are working on.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The /tmp directory is a temporary storage space and should not be used for long-term storage [4].
- Users should ensure that they are not storing large amounts of data in the /tmp directory [4].

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

Dear Sir/Madam,
My name is Guillaume Alexis, id : grfa2. My working directory is /rds/user/grfa2/hpc-work/
For now, I don't have a job submission script. I'm solely trying to install a package named Dedalus ([https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/installation.html](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/installation.html)) and its dependancies, in order to run my script.
My main question is : How should I do it ? I tried in a conda environment, but I'm unsure about the intel/intel-mpi/fftw/hdf5 versions I should load, in order to build Dedalus correctly with these modules.
Thank you for your help,
Best wishes,
Guillaume Alexis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Python Environment Management / Package Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to install Dedalus while relying on the cluster-wide conda module. The supported path is to install a personal Miniconda/Miniforge instance in `hpc-work`, then follow the upstream installation method from that personal environment. The user does not need to manually choose cluster MPI/FFTW module versions if they install the package through the documented conda-forge workflow.

ACTION
1) Do not use the shared cluster conda module for this workflow.
2) Install Miniconda or Miniforge in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Create the environment there and follow the upstream Dedalus installation instructions.
4) Once installed, use Slurm or an interactive job as normal to run the code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Guillaume,

Please avoid using the cluster’s shared conda module for this install. Instead, install your own Miniconda or Miniforge under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create the Dedalus environment there.

If you follow the Dedalus installation guidance from that personal environment, you should not need to manually select Intel MPI/FFTW module versions for the basic conda-forge install path.

Once the environment is ready, you can run the code via Slurm as usual, or use an interactive job if you want to test it first.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer user-managed conda installs in `hpc-work` over the outdated shared conda module.

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an

Dedalus is a flexible (PDE)-solver software that takes the form of a Python 3 package that includes custom C-extensions (compiled with Cython) and that relies on MPI, FFTW (linked

I can build a stand-alone installation but I'd prefer to instead use the modules available on CSD3. 
I was wondering whether Dedalus has been installed for other users and/or if you have some tips on how I should proceed (e.g. is there a virtual environment with all relevant modules I could simply activate?). 
Thank you very much, 
Best 
Louis Couston

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Conor,
Please try to install additional software in your own account yourself. You can install dedalus by following the conda instructions at 
[https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures)
which boils down to this:
```
mkdir ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
cd ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/DedalusProject/dedalus_conda/master/install_conda.sh
# Edit install_conda.sh:
BLAS="mkl"
PYTHON_VERSION="3.7"
# - end edits

module load miniconda/3
conda activate base
bash install_conda.sh
```
This will (at length) create a dedalus conda environment which you can then use by doing
conda activate dedalus
then running
python3 -m dedalus command
but please note the test, bench and cov functions don't work because of some problem in the python itself - some dependency issue not handled correctly by conda. If this method of installation doesn't in fact produce a functioning dedalus then please try one of the other installation methods described on the above web page, but please bear in mind that we can't support every item of 3rd party software that one can download from the internet.
In order to run this thing inside a job script then remember to perform the
module load miniconda/3
conda activate dedalus
commands in your job script.
Best regards,
Stuart

## Running OpenMM on CSD3[¶](#running-openmm-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

To submit an OpenMM job please use the following submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#! Which project should be charged (NB Wilkes2 projects end in '-GPU'):
#SBATCH -A CHANGE_TO_YOUR_ACCOUNT-GPU
#! Change to relevant GPU cluster
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -o openmm.out
#SBATCH -e openmm.err
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=00:10:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
#SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
##SBATCH --no-requeue

#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)

#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by GPU number*walltime.

#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e  's/^\([0-9][0-9]*\).*$/\1/')

Christoforos Hadjigeorgiou commented:
Hi Louis,
For MPI and compilers those are already loaded as part of the default environment.
For HDF5 and FFTW you will also need to load and set some environment variables:
module load fftw-3.3.6-pl2-intel-17.0.4-45kexpw
module load hdf5-1.10.1-intel-17.0.4-acprvzg
export FFTW_PATH=/usr/local/software/spack/spack-0.11.2/opt/spack/linux-rhel7-x86_64/intel-17.0.4/fftw-3.3.6-pl2-45kexpwgmgvqnuq4yse2vjee3hbvxnyb
export FFTW_LIBRARY_PATH=$FFTW_PATH/lib
export FFTW_INCLUDE_PATH=$FFTW_PATH/include
export MPI_PATH=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/intel/2017.4/compilers_and_libraries_2017.4.196/linux/mpi/intel64
export MPI_LIBRARY_PATH=$MPI_PATH/lib
export MPI_INCLUDE_PATH=$MPI_PATH/include
Some environment variables may be unnecessary but I set them to avoid any complains during install.
To install dedalus using pip in the virtual environment:
pip install dedalus
To install dedalus manually I ran while inside the venv:
hg clone [https://bitbucket.org/dedalus-project/dedaluscd](https://bitbucket.org/dedalus-project/dedaluscd) dedalus
pip install -r ./requirements
pip install .
1. These two steps may be unnecessary, you can try running dedalus without them first
 python3 ./setup.py build_ext --inplace 
 python3 ./setup.py install
 
To run dedalus for both pip and manual installation I did:
LD_PRELOAD=$MPI_PATH/lib/libmpi.so python3
Without the preload FFTW complains about not finding MPI functions.
Let us know how it goes.
Kind regards,
Chris

user_input:
Good afternoon,
A student from CEB would like to use abaqus 2024 on the HPC cluster. The student mentioned that they contacted you and you advised that this version is not compatible.
I wanted to confirm if this is the case or did the end user misunderstand.
Kind regards,
Zac
------------------------------------------------------
Zac Iskander (he/him)
IT Support Team Leader
University of Cambridge
Department of Engineering
Trumpington Street
Cambridge, CB2 1PZ, UK
01223760591
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Version Support / Licensing
Technical Action Required: No (policy clarification)
Escalation Required: Yes (new build request if required)
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The immediate answer is that `abaqus/2024` is not currently available as a centrally supported version on the cluster; the latest supported central version is `abaqus/2022`. If there is a requirement for a newer version, licensing and a separate build or install request would need to be coordinated.

ACTION
1) Confirm that `abaqus/2022` is the latest supported central version.
2) Explain that no timeline can be promised for `abaqus/2024`.
3) If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, ask them to submit a fresh build or install request to HPC support.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Zac,

At present the newest centrally supported version on the cluster is `abaqus/2022`, so the user did not misunderstand.

If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, please send a new request to `support@hpc.cam.ac.uk` and we can look into building and providing that version.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Abaqus Compatibility
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is inquiring about using Abaqus 2024 on the HPC cluster, and there is evidence that the version of Abaqus available on the cluster may not be compatible with the user's needs [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To proceed, we need to confirm the compatibility of Abaqus 2024 with the HPC cluster. We recommend checking the official documentation and contacting the Engineering team for specific guidance on accessing the license [3][4]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you have requested access to Abaqus via https://help.eng.cam.ac.uk/software/abaqus/ [3]?
- Are you using a departmental license for Abaqus, and if so, have you discussed the details with your PI [3]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We are looking into the compatibility of Abaqus 2024 with the HPC cluster. To proceed, could you please confirm if you have requested access to Abaqus via https://help.eng.cam.ac.uk/software/abaqus/ [3]? We also recommend discussing the details with your PI if you are using a departmental license [3]. 
Please get back in touch if you have any further questions.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When using licensed products like Abaqus, it is essential to follow the official guidelines and documentation [3][4].
- Users should prioritize official documentation and Service Desk advice over AI-generated suggestions [4].

REFERENCE KEY
[3] : HPCSSUP-91919
[4] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/getting_support.html::chunk::0002

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

Hi Team,
I’m getting in touch regarding an issue on the SRCP platform, which I’ve attached for reference. We urgently need the SNPolisher R package installed into the environment associated with the following platform ID: kjtcs2jvzw7.
This is time‑critical, as the NHSBT Genomics Programme is currently unable to carry out essential clinical work without this package being available.
Could you please prioritise this installation at your earliest opportunity and confirm once it has been completed?
Grateful
[cid:b9547aa4-5bed-4828-8cca-804065ce11a4]
Many thanks,
Justin Ofosu-Dankwa, PhD
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Deployment / R Module
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
This is an urgent request to make the SNPolisher package available on a specific SRCP platform. The correct initial support path is to prioritise escalation to the SRCP/platform team for package installation. Once availability is confirmed, the next step is to validate the package in a plain R session before troubleshooting any surrounding workflow/tool integration.

ACTION
1) Escalate the request to the SRCP/platform team as a priority package-installation request.
2) Once the package is reported available, validate it in a plain R session.
3) If the user's external tool still fails after installation, identify the tool name and R path/environment it uses.
4) Update the user once installation status has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Justin,

We are treating this as a priority request and are arranging installation of SNPolisher with the SRCP/platform team.

Once the package is available, we will send you the relevant load instructions and, if needed, follow up with checks to make sure your workflow is using the correct R environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. # R[¶](#r "Link to this heading")

Like Python, R has its own built-in build system.

The R build system is remarkably uniform and well-tested.
This makes it one of the easiest build systems to create new Spack packages for.

## Phases[¶](#phases "Link to this heading")

The `RBuilder` and `RPackage` base classes have a single phase:

1. `install` - install the package

By default, this phase runs the following command:

```
$ R CMD INSTALL --library=/path/to/installation/prefix/rlib/R/library .
```

## Finding R packages[¶](#finding-r-packages "Link to this heading")

The vast majority of R packages are hosted on CRAN - The Comprehensive R Archive Network.
If you are looking for a particular R package, search for “CRAN <package-name>” and you should quickly find what you want.
If it isn’t on CRAN, try Bioconductor, another common R repository.

For the purposes of this tutorial, we will be walking through [r-caret](https://github.com/spack/spack-packages/blob/develop/repos/spack_repo/builtin/packages/r_caret/package.py) as an example.
If you search for “CRAN caret”, you will quickly find what you are looking for at [https://cran.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cran.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret). <https://cran.r-project.org> is the main CRAN website.
However, CRAN also has a <https://cloud.r-project.org> site that automatically redirects to [mirrors around the world](https://cloud.r-project.org/mirrors.html).
For stability and performance reasons, we will use [https://cloud.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cloud.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret).
If you search for “Package source”, you will find the download URL for the latest release.
Use this URL with `spack create` to create a new package.

## Package name[¶](#package-name "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll notice is that Spack prepends `r-` to the front of the package name.
This is how Spack separates R extensions from the rest of the packages in Spack.
Without this, we would end up with package name collisions more frequently than we would like.
For instance, there are already packages for both:

* `ape` and `r-ape`
* `curl` and `r-curl`
* `gmp` and `r-gmp`
* `jpeg` and `r-jpeg`
* `openssl` and `r-openssl`
* `uuid` and `r-uuid`
* `xts` and `r-xts`

Many popular programs written in C/C++ are later ported to R as a separate project.

## Description[¶](#description "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll need to add to your new package is a description.
The top of the homepage for `caret` lists the following description:

> Classification and Regression Training
>
> Misc functions for training and plotting classification and regression models.

user_input:
Hi
I've been getting an error on Ampere jobs recently where it fails immediately, stating that the CUDA-capable devices aren't available. I've had this error occur when using submission scripts for running turbostream 3.6.10 which has worked for years, and using a separate script running turbostream 4.2.127 recently. I've attached an example slurm.out file I've had from a turbostream4 job. This error seems to happen whenever I get assigned gpu-q-29, though I've heard colleagues mention getting something similar recently on different nodes. I'm aware I can exclude gpu-q-29 in my sbatch, but if it happens on other nodes and I lose all the time I waited in the queue, it's frustrating. Are there any sbatch instructions I can add to avoid this happening, or a fix for the route problem?
Many Thanks,
Dom

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Runtime Failure / Node Health / Slurm Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The job is intermittently failing on a specific GPU node with CUDA device availability errors. Two grounded mitigations from the submitted command/output are to ensure the correct Ampere base module stack is loaded and to let Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm instead of forcing `-npernode` and `-np`.

ACTION
1) Load the correct base stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/default-amp`
2) Remove manual `mpirun -npernode ... -np ...` overrides where possible.
3) Prefer `--tasks-per-node=4` rather than only `--ntasks=8` for the reported two-node case.
4) As a temporary workaround, route around the bad node with:
   `#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`
5) If the issue persists after these changes or starts affecting other nodes, escalate for node-health investigation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Dom,

Please first make sure your script loads `rhel8/default-amp`, as that adds the expected CUDA runtime environment.

We also recommend removing the explicit `-npernode`/`-np` arguments and letting Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm. For a two-node, eight-task job, `--tasks-per-node=4` is the safer Slurm-side request.

In the short term, you can avoid the problematic node with:
`#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Thank you for reporting the issue with CUDA-capable devices being busy or unavailable on the Ampere nodes. We recommend checking the job submission script to ensure that the --gres=gpu directive is used to specify the number of GPUs per node. If the issue persists, please try rebooting the node or resubmitting the job. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always specify the number of GPUs per node using the --gres=gpu directive to avoid conflicts [2][5][6].
- Be aware of the node availability and technical issues before submitting jobs [8].

## Recommendations for running on ampere[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

Since the gpu-q nodes are running Rocky8, you will want to recompile
your applications. We suggest you do this by requesting an
[interactive node](#software).

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

The per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for ampere[¶](#default-submission-script-for-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Ampere default job submission script modified for the ampere nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs, but can be modified for other types
of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts, please see
the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.

The slurmd daemon maintains a list of the jobs which have already been
terminated to avoid replay of task launch requests.
If the slurmctld daemon is cold-started (with the "-c" option
or "/etc/init.d/slurm startclean"), it starts job ID values

If the slurmd is not also cold-started, it will reject job launch requests
for jobs that it considers terminated.
This solution to this problem is to cold-start all slurmd daemons whenever
the slurmctld daemon is cold-started.

**"Unable to accept new connection: Too many open
files"**
The srun command automatically increases its open file limit to
the hard limit in order to process all of the standard input and output
connections to the launched tasks. It is recommended that you set the
open file hard limit to 8192 across the cluster.

***SlurmdDebug* fails to log job step information
at the appropriate level**
There are two programs involved here. One is **slurmd**, which is
a persistent daemon running at the desired debug level. The second
program is **slurmstepd**, which executes the user job and its
debug level is controlled by the user. Submitting the job with
an option of *--debug=#* will result in the desired level of
detail being logged in the *SlurmdLogFile* plus the output
of the program.

**"Batch JobId=# missing from batch node <node>
(not found BatchStartTime after startup)"**
A shell is launched on node zero of a job's allocation to execute
the submitted program. The *slurmd* daemon executing on each compute
node will periodically report to the *slurmctld* what programs it
is executing. If a batch program is expected to be running on some
node (i.e. node zero of the job's allocation) and is not found, the
message above will be logged and the job canceled. This typically is
associated with exhausting memory on the node or some other critical
failure that cannot be recovered from.

PMIx uses the **hwloc API** for different purposes, including
*OS device* features like querying sysfs folders (such as
*/sys/class/net* and */sys/class/infiniband*) to get the names of
Infiniband HCAs. With the above mentioned features, hwloc defaults to
querying the OpenCL devices, which creates handles on */dev/nvidia\** files.
These handles are kept by slurmstepd and will result in the following error
inside a job:

```
$ nvidia-smi mig --id 1 --create-gpu-instance FOO,FOO --default-compute-instance
Unable to create a GPU instance on GPU 1 using profile FOO: In use by another client
```

In order to use Multi-Instance GPUs with Slurm and PMIx you can instruct hwloc
to not query OpenCL devices by setting the
HWLOC\_COMPONENTS=-opencl environment
variable for slurmd, i.e. setting this variable in systemd unit file for slurmd.

## GPU Management

In the case of Slurm's GRES plugin for GPUs, the environment variable
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
is set for each job step to determine which GPUs are
available for its use on each node. This environment variable is only set
when tasks are launched on a specific compute node (no global environment
variable is set for the *salloc* command and the environment variable set
for the *sbatch* command only reflects the GPUs allocated to that job
on that node, node zero of the allocation).
CUDA version 3.1 (or higher) uses this environment
variable in order to run multiple jobs or job steps on a node with GPUs
and ensure that the resources assigned to each are unique. In the example
above, the allocated node may have four or more graphics devices. In that
case, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
will reference unique devices for each file and
the output might resemble this:

```
JobStep=1234.0 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1
JobStep=1234.1 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2
JobStep=1234.2 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3
```

**NOTE**: Be sure to specify the *File* parameters in the
*gres.conf* file and ensure they are in the increasing numeric order.

The `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
environment variable will also be set in the job's Prolog and Epilog programs.
Note that the environment variable set for the job may differ from that set for
the Prolog and Epilog if Slurm is configured to constrain the device files
visible to a job using Linux cgroup.
This is because the Prolog and Epilog programs run outside of any Linux
cgroup while the job runs inside of the cgroup and may thus have a
different set of visible devices.
For example, if a job is allocated the device "/dev/nvidia1", then
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a value of
"1" in the Prolog and Epilog while the job's value of
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a
value of "0" (i.e. the first GPU device visible to the job).
For more information see the
[Prolog and Epilog Guide](prolog_epilog.html).

When possible, Slurm automatically determines the GPUs on the system using
NVML. NVML (which powers the
`nvidia-smi` tool) numbers GPUs in order by their
PCI bus IDs. user_input:
Dear HPC team,
Please find attached a purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2 , to be credited to Emily Lines.
Best wishes,
Anne Baranger
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SL2 Credit Provisioning / Procurement Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The requester has sent a PO for SL2 CPU hours to be credited for Emily Lines. From the initial message alone, it is not clear whether these hours should go onto an existing SL2 project or whether a new project account is needed. The correct next step is to confirm the target SL2 account and, if none exists, follow the normal project-creation and approval path.

ACTION
1) Ask whether the credits are for an existing SL2 project or a new one.
2) If no suitable existing SL2 CPU project exists, follow the normal approval process to create one.
3) Add the required users to the target project once the account path is confirmed.
4) If an existing account is intended instead, request the exact target project code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Anne,

Before we can apply the purchased CPU hours, we need to confirm whether they are for an existing SL2 CPU project or whether a new project needs to be created.

If there is no suitable existing SL2 CPU project for this work, we would need to follow the normal approval process before creating one and applying the hours.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC Account Management [3]
Technical Action Required: Yes [3]
Portal Action Required: No [3]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has provided a purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2 to be credited to Emily Lines [3]. This is a standard procedure for adding compute hours to a user's account.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the purchase order and ensure it is complete and valid [3].
2. Use the `makecpu` command to add the CPU hours to the user's account, following the format used in previous tickets [5].
3. Update the account settings and associations as necessary [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please confirm the exact project name and ID for Emily Lines?
- Are there any specific requirements or constraints for the CPU hours being added?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your purchase order for 2,000 CPU hours of service level 2. We will process this request and add the hours to Emily Lines' account.
Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all purchase orders are verified and valid before processing [3].
- Follow established procedures for adding compute hours to user accounts [5].

REFERENCE KEY
[3] : HPCSSUP-73957
[5] : HPCSSUP-89499

staff within the University.

The service allows a range of service levels with different features. These are associated with different quality of service definitions within the scheduler, enforcing different job priorities, maximum job sizes and maximum run times.

A service level is attached to a project, which is a group of users led by a principal investigator (PI) who controls (or who can apply for) a line of funding for HPC.

## Service Levels[¶](#service-levels "Permalink to this headline")

The current Service Levels (SLs), which came into operation in November 2017 and which apply to the Peta4 and Wilkes3 clusters, are described below.

Funding units are in the form of usage credits. What these credits represent depends on the type of cluster. For the CPU cluster (Peta4) 1 credit = 1 CPU core for 1 hour. For the GPU cluster (Wilkes3) 1 credit = 1 GPU for 1 hour.

Accounting periods or quarters are three month periods of the year running 1st February - 30th April, 1st May - 31st July, 1st August - 31st October and 1st November - 31st January.

## Service Level 1 – Guaranteed Usage[¶](#service-level-1-guaranteed-usage "Permalink to this headline")

Service Level 1 (SL1) operates with the highest quality of service QOS1, designed for groups which require large and steady amounts of computer time over a long period.

Funds paid will be converted into core hour credits. ## Service Level 3 – Free Usage[¶](#service-level-3-free-usage "Permalink to this headline")

Service Level 3 (SL3) operates with the medium quality of service, QOS2. QOS2 is lower than QOS1 which is used in SL1 & SL2. This service level is designed for groups with medium usage requirements who currently do not have funding to pay for their usage, thus an immediate conversion of funds into credits is not required.

SL3 is capped with a maximum number of usage credits per PI per quarter. This has been introduced to promote a more even usage of the free time on the system. Currently each PI may receive **200,000 CPU core hours** and **3000 GPU hours** per quarter.

There is no guaranteed minimum usage level for SL3 and there is no concept of expiry or of moving core hours across quarters.

Once a group in SL3 has consumed all its allowed core hours in a quarter, jobs can still be submitted at Service Level 4.

This lists the charge for each job in the CompHrs column. Since this example queries usage of a -CPU project, these are CPU core hours. Similarly, for a -GPU project they would be GPU hours.

**I would like to add core hours to a particular member of my group**

```
gdeposit -z 10000 -p halos-sl2-spqr1-gpu
```

This coordinator of the HALOS-SL2-GPU might use this to add 10000 GPU hours to the HALOS-SL2-SPQR1-GPU subproject assigned to the user spqr1. Note that if a compute hour limit applies to the parent of the project in the project hierarchy - i.e. if the parent project HALOS-SL2-GPU has an overall compute hour limit (which it almost certainly does) - then the global limit will still apply across all per-user projects.

Compute hours may be added to a project by a designated project coordinator user. Reducing the compute hours available to a project is also possible by adding a negative number of hours via the –time= syntax, e.g. the following command undoes the above:

```
gdeposit --time=-10000 -p halos-sl2-spqr1-gpu
```

user_input:
Hi there,
I manage a Research Data store for the “kstarkstar” project:
Directory: rds-mk652-kstarkstar
Project ID: bUoO2gzww9Q
It is about to expire. I want to extend the license for another year (for all 5TB) with all of the same permissions. I raised a PO have requested a new data store but think I may have made a mistake here. I don’t want a new storage area, I just want to extend the existing one.
Please can you resolve my mistake?
The PO for the extension is attached.
Many thanks,
Matt
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Renewal / Procurement Correction
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants to extend an existing storage project but believes they may have accidentally requested a new storage purchase instead. The safe handling is to stop them from activating anything new until support has verified which service record should be extended and whether the mistaken request can be corrected or needs to be cancelled or reissued.

ACTION
1) Ask the user not to activate the mistaken newly purchased storage project.
2) Confirm which existing project is the one that should be extended.
3) Check internally whether the mistaken request can be removed or converted.
4) If cancellation or reissue is required, direct the user to the appropriate purchasing or finance route.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Matt,

Please leave the accidentally purchased storage project unallocated while we verify whether the mistaken request can be corrected or whether it needs to be cancelled and reissued.

Once we confirm the correct path, we will tell you whether any purchasing or finance action is needed on your side.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Licence Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [1][2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has raised a purchase order for a new Research Data Store (RDS) but intends to extend the existing storage instead [1]. The existing storage project is rds-mk652-kstarkstar with a project ID of bUoO2gzww9Q [1]. The user wants to extend the license for another year with the same permissions and 5TB capacity [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Delete the new RDS storage request as it was submitted in error [7].
2. Ask the user to upload the purchase order against the storage extension for the particular storage they wish to extend [7].
3. Verify that the license for the current storage is extended correctly and matches the user's requirements [1][7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the exact purchase order number and date for the storage extension?
- Are there any specific permissions or access controls that need to be maintained during the extension process?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We will delete the new RDS storage request and ask you to upload the purchase order against the storage extension for rds-mk652-kstarkstar. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the correct storage project is extended to avoid data loss or unauthorized access [1][2].
- Verify that the purchase order is valid and correctly uploaded to the self-service portal [7].

Important

The School of Biological Sciences ‘SBS’ resource allocation scheme ended on 31 Dec 2022.

Any PIs and users still wishing to retain their current storage projects should respond to the automated renewal notices sent from the self-service portal prior to 31 Dec. Or alternatively, contact the servicedesk at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance in identifying the relevant projects or migrating the data elsewhere.

## Key Deadlines[¶](#key-deadlines "Permalink to this headline")

### From 1 January 2023[¶](#from-1-january-2023 "Permalink to this headline")

* Any storage projects not yet renewed have been flagged as ‘suspended’ in the
  storage portal.
* Data owners and managers can still generate quotes and submit purchase orders to renew,
  which will be automatically backdated to be effective from 1 Jan.
* Access to data is still possible during the 30 day grace period for late renewals,
  after which access to the data will be lost.

### From 30 January 2023 (end of grace period)[¶](#from-30-january-2023-end-of-grace-period "Permalink to this headline")

* User access to unrenewed projects will be lost.
* All data in projects will **only be accessible on request**.
  Access will be provided in a read-only mode in cases where it is necessary to migrate
  data to an alternative location on CSD3 or offsite.

### From 1 July 2023[¶](#from-1-july-2023 "Permalink to this headline")

Warning

The WBIC platform contained a number of different storage areas for
users, all of which will be **retired** with the closure of the platform.

All data should be migrated to an alternative storage platform on
CSD3 or downloaded to another location before the platform is
shut-down.

Below we outline alternative services on CSD3 for each storage area that
are available for purchase so that you can replicate the WBIC
environment as best as possible.

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.
